data.title: Super Micro: Buy The Fear
data.description:
Despite forecasting booming AI server demand, Super Micro Computer, Inc. (NASDAQ:SMCI) is slumping nearly 20% following earnings. The AI infrastructure company has a lot of doubters due to the delayed 10-K, a management overhaul, and missed financial targets. My investment thesis is ultra bullish on the stock, especially on a further dip towards $40, as Super Micro is cheap based on the AI opportunity ahead.

Another Shortfall

Super Micro has constantly run into capital constraints, and the 10-K delay fed into expected volatile results for the June quarter. The company reported the following FQ4'25 results:

The company reported a FQ4 revenue miss of $110 million, but this isn't anything new. Super Micro missed FQ3 targets by $135 million and missed every quarterly analyst estimate in FY25 while growing revenues 50% during the year.

While Super Micro has struggled in the last few quarters, the June quarter revenues were not far off record revenues reported in FQ1 at $5.94 billion. The market clearly doesn't trust the company following the delayed 10-K filing, so Super Micro will need to hit FY26 targets in order to regain confidence.

On the FQ1 earnings call, CEO Charles Liang highlighted the problems leading to the revenue shortfall as follows:

Allow me to go a little deeper at the June revenue shortfall in what was otherwise a stronger quarter. Shortfall stem from 2 key factors: a capital constraint that limited our ability to rapidly scale production and specification changes from a major new customer that delay revenue recognition because of new ad of some new ad features.

Super Micro completed a $2.3 billion convertible debt offering at the end of June. Clearly, this offering solved the capital constraint issue with the company ending June with a cash balance of $5.2 billion plus $4.7 billion in inventories.

The company announced the business is growing from 4 large-scale datacenter customers to 6 to 8 in FY26, another sign the business will take off in the quarters ahead. In addition, the business has shifted away from U.S. customers, with Asia growing 91% to 42% of total sales, reducing some of the headline risk U.S. investors see with domestic competitors.

Big EPS Upside

The company only reported a gross margin of 9.5% in FQ4 with a dip from 10.2% last year. Super Micro was expected to boost margins as the business expanded sales due to massive AI demand, but reduced sales from delayed customer orders naturally impacted expected financials.

Due to 10-k delays, Super Micro has pulled back from the up to $40 billion FY26 revenue target. The company is still targeting at least $33 billion (opening the door to still hitting $40 billion), while the guidance for FQ1 is a record $6 to $7 billion.

Super Micro guided to FY26 revenues of at least $33 billion. The company has the following basic financial models for FY26:

14% Gross Margin

  • Revenue: $33 billion
  • Gross profit: 14%
  • Operating expense: 4%
  • Taxes: 15.5%
  • Net income: $2.79 billion
  • EPS (644 million share): $4.33

17% Gross Margin

  • Revenue: $40 billion
  • Gross margin: 17%
  • Operating expense: 4%
  • Taxes: 15.5%
  • Net income: $4.39 billion
  • EPS (644 million shares): $6.82

The major assumption is that gross margins rebound to the previous forecasts of 14% to 17%, with FY24 sitting at nearly 14%. The company built the data center building block solution (DCBBS) in order to move beyond a price war on rack servers and provide a higher margin solution to help customers get online quicker.

The above financial works out the range of possibilities from the low-end 14% gross margin in FY26 on revenues of $33 billion and the high-end of 17% on $40 billion in revenue. The EPS targets jump from $4.33 to $6.82, as a few percentage points on margins have a big impact on earnings. The company will start FY26 at closer to 10% gross margins as the DCBBS gets started, making the goal more of a normalized goal than a specific number for this year.

The consensus analyst EPS estimates are down at only $2.75 for FY26, with a jump to $3.49 in FY27. The analyst numbers provide an easy hurdle with a FY26 revenue target of only $30 billion.

Super Micro definitely faces a lot of risk. A few more quarters of missing financial targets would be a huge negative for the stock, while the gross margin expansion targets will be difficult to ever meet while missing targets.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that the market is selling off Super Micro, but the company appears back on track to ride the AI wave much higher. The AI server solutions provider clearly needs to report a quarterly beat for FQ1 to regain investor confidence, but once this happens, the stock could trade far higher.
